html:
[1]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 庚巳编 - Title meaning 'Compilation of the Gengsi Year.' '庚巳' is a combination of two heavenly stems (Geng and Si) used in the Chinese sexagenary cycle to denote a specific year. The term may also be used as a literary title, not necessarily referring to a specific historical year. '编' means 'compilation' or 'collection.'
  • 撰 - Term meaning 'compiled' or 'authored,' often used in classical Chinese to denote the authorship of a text.
  • 目录 - Direct translation of 'table of contents' or 'index.' It is a standard term used in classical Chinese texts to denote the list of chapters or sections.
  • 圣瑞 - Compound term meaning 'divine signs' or 'sacred omens.' It refers to extraordinary events or phenomena considered to be signs from heaven or deities.
  • 太学 - Refers to the 'Imperial Academy' or 'National University,' an ancient institution of higher learning in China.
  • 贡院 - 'Tribute hall' or 'examination hall,' where candidates for the imperial examinations (科举) took their tests.
  • 平保儿 - Likely a name or title, possibly of a person or a local figure. The term '保儿' may be a colloquial or regional term for a child or youth, but without further context, it remains uncertain.
  • 袁珙 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical figure or scholar. '袁' is a surname, and '珙' is a given name.
  • 登科先兆 - 'Omen of being admitted to the imperial examination.' It refers to signs or predictions of success in the exams.
  • 揣骨僧 - 'Bone-reading monk' or 'physiognomist monk,' a term referring to a monk who practiced divination by examining bones or physical features.
  • 沅江麟 - A name or title, possibly of a mythical creature (麒麟, qilin) associated with the沅江 (Yuan River), a river in Hunan province.
  • 豢龙 - A term referring to 'raising dragons' or 'dragon-keeping,' which may be a metaphor for controlling power or an allusion to a mythological practice.
  • 兖州岳庙 - A temple dedicated to 'Yue' (a deity or mountain god) in the city of Yanzhou (兖州), likely a local shrine or place of worship.
  • 王全 - A proper name, possibly of a person or official.
  • 鬼兵 - 'Ghost soldiers' or 'spectral troops,' a term used to describe supernatural armies or apparitions.
  • 守银犬 - 'Guardian silver dog' or 'dog that guards silver,' possibly a metaphorical or supernatural entity.
  • 村民遇土地 - 'Villagers meeting the land god,' referring to an encounter with a local deity or spirit.
  • 竹圃异物 - 'Strange things in the bamboo grove,' referring to unusual or supernatural occurrences in a bamboo field.
  • 空同山人 - A term meaning 'man of Kungtong Mountain,' likely a title or pseudonym used by a scholar or writer.
  • 张秋帛烬 - A name or title; '张' is a surname, '秋' may refer to autumn or a name, and '帛烬' could mean 'ashes of silk' or 'burned silk.'
  • 王絙 - A proper name; '絙' is a rare character, possibly meaning 'rope' or 'cord,' but in this context, it is likely a personal name.
  • 灵芝 - 'Spiritual mushroom' or 'ginseng mushroom,' a type of fungus considered to have mystical or medicinal properties.
  • 马鬼 - 'Horse ghost' or 'spectral horse,' referring to a supernatural or ghostly horse.
  • 三足鳖 - 'Three-legged turtle,' a mythical creature or a rare natural occurrence.
  • 洞箫记 - 'Record of the hollow flute,' likely a title of a story or account involving a flute or music.
  • 普光伽蓝 - A temple or shrine, possibly dedicated to the Buddha or a deity. '伽蓝' refers to a Buddhist temple or the guardian deities of a temple.
  • 方学 - A term meaning 'square learning' or 'rectangular studies,' possibly referring to a school or a method of study.
  • 七总管部使 - A title or position, possibly referring to an official who oversaw seven departments or regions.
  • 周岐凤 - A proper name; '周' is a surname, '岐凤' may be a name or title.
  • 柴驿丞 - A title for a local official or clerk in charge of a posthouse or驿站 (cháiyì), a station along the road for travelers.
  • 罗江神祠 - A shrine or temple dedicated to a deity associated with the Luo River or a local area.
  • 戚编修 - A title for an official who worked in the imperial compilation office ('编修'). '戚' is a surname.
  • 临江狐 - 'Fox of the riverbank,' likely referring to a story or encounter with a fox spirit near a river.
  • 果报 - 'Karmic retribution' or 'fate,' a term from Buddhist and Daoist thought referring to the consequences of actions.
  • 鸡变 - 'Chicken transformation,' possibly a story about a chicken changing into a human or a mythical creature.
  • 剑池 - 'Sword Pool' or 'Sword Pond,' likely a location or a name associated with swords or weapons.
  • 西山狐 - 'Fox of the western mountain,' similar to '临江狐,' referring to a fox spirit or supernatural entity.
  • 程学士降笔 - 'The descent of the brush by Master Cheng,' likely referring to a story where a scholar receives divine inspiration or writes under supernatural influence.
  • 蒋生 - A proper name, likely of a scholar or student.
  • 盛氏怪 - 'Strange occurrences of the Sheng family,' referring to a family experiencing supernatural events.
  • 人为牛 - 'Man turned into a cow,' likely a transformation story or a myth.
  • 袁尚宝 - A proper name; '尚宝' may refer to a title or honorific.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 庚巳编 - This title may be a reference to a literary work or a historical record. The term '庚巳' is part of the Chinese sexagenary cycle, which is used for dating and divination. However, without more context, it is unclear whether this is a specific historical year or a symbolic title.
  • 太学 - The Imperial Academy, a central institution of higher learning in ancient China, established during the Han dynasty. It was the highest educational institution in the empire.
  • 贡院 - The examination halls where candidates for the imperial civil service exams (科举) took their tests. These were crucial for social mobility in imperial China.
  • 袁珙 - A real historical figure; Yuan Gao (袁珙) was a famous physiognomist and physician of the Yuan and Ming dynasties. He was known for his ability to predict people's fates based on their appearance.
  • 沅江 - A river in Hunan province. It is also a cultural and mythological reference in Chinese literature.
  • 兖州 - A city in Shandong province. It was an important administrative and cultural center in ancient China.
  • 土地 - The 'land god,' a local deity worshipped by Chinese villagers to protect their land and crops.
  • 灵芝 - A sacred mushroom believed to have healing properties and to be a sign of good fortune or divine favor.
  • 三足鳖 - The three-legged turtle is a mythological creature in Chinese tradition, often associated with longevity, power, or the divine.
  • 洞箫 - A type of flute made from a hollow bamboo tube. It is a traditional Chinese instrument used in folk and classical music.
  • 伽蓝 - A term used in Buddhism to refer to the guardian deities of a temple or monastery. It can also refer to the temple itself.
  • 编修 - A title for a scholar employed in the imperial court to compile official records, histories, or texts. It was a prestigious position in the Hanlin Academy.
  • 临江 - A place name, possibly referring to a river or region near a body of water. It may be a reference to a specific location where a supernatural event occurred.
  • 果报 - A concept from Buddhism and Daoism that emphasizes the idea of karmic consequences. Actions in life determine one's fate in this world or the next.
  • 鸡变 - This may be a reference to a myth or folktale about a chicken transforming into a human or a supernatural being, similar to other transformation myths in Chinese folklore.
  • 剑池 - The 'Sword Pool' is a legendary location associated with the famous swordsman and philosopher Zengzi (曾子), or it may refer to a location where swords were stored or forged.

Proper Nouns

  • 袁珙 - A historical figure; Yuan Gao (袁珙) was a famous physiognomist and physician of the Yuan and Ming dynasties.
  • 沅江 - A river in Hunan province.
  • 兖州 - A city in Shandong province.
  • 袁尚宝 - A proper name; '尚宝' may refer to a title or honorific.
  • 蒋生 - A proper name, likely of a scholar or student.
  • 罗江 - A river or region associated with the shrine or temple.
  • 戚 - A surname, as in '戚编修.'
  • 程学士 - A proper name; '程' is a surname, and '学士' is a title for a scholar.
  • 盛氏 - A family name, as in '盛氏怪.'
  • 王全 - A proper name, possibly of a person or official.
  • 王絙 - A proper name; '絙' is a rare character, possibly meaning 'rope' or 'cord,' but in this context, it is likely a personal name.
  • 张秋 - A proper name; '张' is a surname, and '秋' may refer to autumn or a name.

[2]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 還金童子 - Literal translation: 'the child who returned gold'; may refer to a story or case where a child returned lost gold, often used in classical texts as a moral exemplum or anecdote.
  • 户婚亲中司 - Bureaucratic or administrative title, possibly referring to an official in charge of household, marriage, and kinship matters.
  • 古铜鸭盆 - Ancient bronze duck basin; likely refers to an object or artifact, possibly symbolic or used in a ritual or anecdote.
  • 侍女峯 - Attendant girl Peak; 峯 is a variant of 峰 (peak), which may be a name or a metaphor for someone prominent among attendants.
  • 蛇癫 - Snake madness or serpent madness; could refer to a medical condition, a supernatural affliction, or a metaphor for erratic behavior.
  • 苟毕元帅 - General Gou Bi; may be a name of a historical or legendary general.
  • 顾镇 - A name, possibly of a person or place.
  • 王栾 - A name, likely a person.
  • 猪犬生儿 - Pig and dog giving birth to a child; may refer to a supernatural or miraculous birth involving animals.
  • 梓潼神 - Zhitong God; associated with literature and examinations in Chinese tradition, often venerated as the god of study.
  • 妇人生须 - A woman growing a beard; may refer to a case of a woman displaying masculine traits, possibly through supernatural or medical means.
  • 黑眚 - Black calamity or black omen; may refer to a supernatural or ominous event, possibly a ghost or demon.
  • 火灾 - Fire disaster; a straightforward term for a fire incident.
  • 王士能 - A name, likely of a person.
  • 王主簿 - Mr. Wang, the chief clerk or clerk of the office; *主簿* was a bureaucratic title in ancient China.
  • 人魂出游 - Human soul wandering or the soul leaving the body; refers to a spiritual or supernatural phenomenon.
  • 肉芝 - Flesh mushroom or meat fungus; a term for a type of medicinal fungus or a metaphor for something rare and valuable.
  • 郑灏 - A name, possibly of a person.
  • 蒋子修 - A name, likely of a person.
  • 黑鱼 - Black fish; a literal term, possibly used in a story or as a symbol.
  • 青虎 - Green tiger; may refer to a tiger with green fur or a symbolic creature.
  • 黄长子 - Yellow eldest son; likely a name or a reference to the eldest son of a family with the surname *Huang*.
  • 雄鸡卵 - Male chicken egg; a paradoxical term, possibly referring to a miraculous or unnatural egg.
  • 沈镗 - A name, likely of a person.
  • 鸡精 - Chicken spirit or spirit of the chicken; may refer to a supernatural entity or a personification of a chicken.
  • 如公 - A name, possibly of a person or a title.
  • 戴妇见死儿 - Mrs. Dai sees the dead child; likely a story or anecdote about a woman encountering a dead child, possibly supernatural.
  • 玄坛黑虎 - Black tiger of the Xuantan; *Xuantan* may refer to a place, a deity, or a ritual space.
  • 钱蛇 - Money snake or coin snake; may refer to a snake associated with money, or a metaphor for something related to wealth or greed.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 户婚亲中司 - Government office or department responsible for household affairs, marriage, and kinship; existed in various dynasties, especially in the Tang and Song periods.
  • 梓潼神 - Zhitong God; deity associated with scholars, venerated by students preparing for the imperial examinations; also called *Zhitong Laojun* or *Wenxuan*.
  • 黑眚 - Black omen or supernatural occurrence; *眚* (sheng) refers to an omen or supernatural event in classical Chinese.
  • 人魂出游 - Related to the concept of *hun* (soul) and *po* (body); the idea that the soul can leave the body is found in Daoist and Buddhist texts.
  • 肉芝 - Rare and valuable medicinal fungus; associated with longevity and immortality in Daoist tradition.
  • 雄鸡卵 - Reference to a miraculous or unnatural event, possibly involving the transformation of a male chicken into a female or the production of an egg by a male bird.
  • 玄坛黑虎 - Refers to a specific deity or supernatural entity; *Xuantan* may refer to a Daoist ritual space or a deity associated with the Black Tiger, one of the four directional guardians in Daoist cosmology.

Proper Nouns

  • 苟毕元帅 - Name of a general or commander; *苟毕* is a name, and *元帅* is a title meaning 'general' or 'commander'.
  • 顾镇 - A name, possibly of a historical figure or a character in the text.
  • 王栾 - A name, possibly of a historical figure or a character.
  • 郑灏 - A name, possibly of a historical figure or a character.
  • 蒋子修 - A name, likely of a historical figure or a character.
  • 沈镗 - A name, likely of a historical figure or a character.
  • 如公 - A name, possibly of a historical figure or a character.
  • 戴妇 - A name, likely referring to a woman named *Dai*.
  • 梓潼神 - A deity, specifically the *Zhitong God*, associated with literature and examinations.
  • 玄坛黑虎 - Refers to a specific deity or supernatural entity, possibly associated with Daoist cosmology.
  • 钱蛇 - A term that may refer to a specific type of snake or a symbol in the text.

[3]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 鬼还家 - Literal meaning 'ghost returns home,' likely refers to a ghost returning to its place of origin or rest, possibly in a metaphorical or supernatural context.
  • 牛偿负钱 - Literal meaning 'cow repays borrowed money,' suggests a story or proverb about a cow repaying a debt, possibly with moral or supernatural implications.
  • 九仙梦验 - Literal meaning 'nine immortals' dream verified,' likely refers to a dream involving nine immortals that was later confirmed or fulfilled.
  • 神丹 - Literal meaning 'divine elixir,' likely refers to a magical or alchemical potion.
  • 鹦鹉山茶 - Literal meaning 'parrot mountain tea,' likely refers to a type of tea or a story involving a parrot and tea.
  • 铁冠道人 - Literal meaning 'Iron-Cap Daoist,' likely refers to a Daoist practitioner with an iron cap, possibly a legendary figure.
  • 五足牛 - Literal meaning 'five-legged cow,' likely refers to a cow with an extra limb, possibly a supernatural or medical anomaly.
  • 变鬼 - Literal meaning 'transformed ghost,' likely refers to a ghost that has changed form or identity.
  • 海岛马人 - Literal meaning 'island horse-man,' likely refers to a person or creature with the features of a horse and a human.
  • 蜂化促织 - Literal meaning 'bee transformed into cricket,' likely refers to a story about a bee turning into a cricket, possibly with moral or supernatural implications.
  • 人痾 - Literal meaning 'human disease,' likely refers to a disease affecting humans, possibly with supernatural or medical significance.
  • 谷亭狐 - Literal meaning 'Gutting Fox,' likely refers to a fox associated with the place named Gutting.
  • 真武显应 - Literal meaning 'True Martial Manifestation,' likely refers to a manifestation or appearance of the deity Zhenwu.
  • 牛祸 - Literal meaning 'cow disaster,' likely refers to a disaster or misfortune involving a cow.
  • 白乌 - Literal meaning 'white crow,' likely refers to a crow with white feathers, possibly a rare or supernatural bird.
  • 方卵猕猴 - Literal meaning 'square egg macaque,' likely refers to a macaque with a square-shaped egg, possibly a supernatural or medical anomaly.
  • 雀报 - Literal meaning 'sparrow report,' likely refers to a story or event involving sparrows.
  • 异人占星 - Literal meaning 'strange person's star divination,' likely refers to a person with unusual abilities who practices astrology or star divination.
  • 奇疾 - Literal meaning 'strange disease,' likely refers to a disease with unusual or supernatural characteristics.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 王端毅公 - Refers to a historical figure, likely a notable person from the past.
  • 岳武穆祠 - Refers to a temple or shrine dedicated to Yue Fei, a famous general and national hero from the Song Dynasty.
  • 关公 - Refers to Guan Yu, a famous general and deity in Chinese culture.
  • 刘公望气 - Refers to a historical figure known for his ability to observe or interpret signs (气, qi).
  • 真武显应 - Refers to the deity Zhenwu, a prominent figure in Chinese Daoism.
  • 楚巫 - Refers to a shaman or witch from the Chu region, a historical and cultural area in China.
  • 杨宽 - Refers to a historical or legendary figure, possibly a scholar or official.
  • 盛御医 - Refers to a renowned physician from the past.

[4]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 卷十 - Refers to the tenth chapter or section of a larger work.
  • 升遐之兆 - A euphemism for signs of impending death or the passing of a great person.
  • 誠意伯 - A title, possibly referring to a high-ranking official or noble.
  • 上梁日時 - Refers to the auspicious day and time for the ritual of placing the ridgepole in a building.
  • 義象行 - Likely refers to a specific event or action related to righteousness or justice.
  • 張潮 - A proper name, possibly a person or a historical figure.
  • 王貫 - Another proper name, likely a person or a historical figure.
  • 斗神救灾 - Refers to the intervention of a celestial deity in disaster relief.
  • 張氏子入冥 - Refers to a story or event where a child of the Zhang family enters the afterlife.
  • 楊少卿 - A proper name, possibly a person or a historical figure.
  • 張孟介 - Another proper name, likely a person or a historical figure.
  • 嚴尚书 - Refers to a high-ranking official or noble, possibly a minister.
  • 木妖 - Refers to a supernatural entity or spirit associated with wood.
  • 唐■〈王巳〉 - A proper name, possibly a person or a historical figure. The character ■〈王巳〉 is unclear and may need further research.
  • 張都宪 - Refers to a high-ranking official or noble, possibly a vice minister.
  • 辟疟镜 - Refers to a mirror or object believed to ward off malaria.
  • 臂龙 - Refers to a dragon or dragon-like entity associated with the arm.
  • 九尾龟 - Refers to a mythical creature with nine tails.
  • 鄱阳水神 - Refers to a water deity associated with the Poyang Lake region.
  • 椶三舍人 - Refers to a person or official, possibly a scholar or scribe.
  • 马少师 - Refers to a high-ranking official or noble, possibly a teacher or mentor.
  • 张御史神政记 - Refers to a record or account of a magistrate's divine governance.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 庚巳编卷第一 - Refers to the first volume of a work titled 'Geng Si Bian'.
  • 太学 - Refers to the Imperial Academy, the highest institution of learning in ancient China.
  • 贡院 - Refers to the examination hall where the imperial examinations were held.
  • 袁珙 - A famous astrologer and diviner from the Ming dynasty.
  • 登科先兆 - Refers to signs or omens predicting success in the imperial examinations.
  • 揣骨僧 - Refers to a monk who practices bone divination, a form of fortune-telling.

Proper Nouns

  • 黄村匠人 - A craftsman from Huangcun.
  • 吴恂 - A proper name, possibly a person or a historical figure.
  • 北斗经 - Refers to a text or scripture related to the Big Dipper constellation.
  • 人瑞 - Refers to a person of exceptional virtue or longevity.
  • 犬精 - Refers to a spirit or supernatural entity associated with dogs.
  • 雷谴道士 - Refers to a Taoist priest who is summoned by thunder.
  • 曾状元 - Refers to a person who achieved the highest rank in the imperial examinations.
  • 腾冲龙 - Refers to a dragon associated with Tengchong.
  • 陨石 - Refers to a meteorite.
  • 尤弘远 - A proper name, possibly a person or a historical figure.
  • 猫王 - Refers to a king or ruler associated with cats.
  • 昭陵银兔 - Refers to a silver rabbit associated with the Zhaoling mausoleum.
  • 梁泽 - A proper name, possibly a person or a historical figure.
  • 黑厮 - Refers to a black servant or slave.
  • 蝎魔 - Refers to a demonic entity associated with scorpions.
  • 胡弘 - A proper name, possibly a person or a historical figure.
  • 瑞莲 - Refers to a lotus flower of good omen.
  • 人妖公案 - Refers to a case or story involving human and demon interactions.

[5]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 豢龍 - Metaphorical term referring to the mythical practice of raising dragons, often used to describe extraordinary or mystical phenomena.
  • 兖州岳庙 - Refers to a temple dedicated to a mountain deity in the state of Yan (兖州), indicating a specific religious or cultural site.
  • 王全 - A personal name, likely of a historical or legendary figure.
  • 鬼兵 - Refers to an army of ghosts or supernatural beings, often associated with folklore or ghost stories.
  • 守银犬 - Literally 'guardian silver dog,' referring to a mythical or protective canine figure.
  • 村民遇土地 - Describes an encounter between a villager and the local land deity, a common theme in Chinese folk religion.
  • 竹圃异物 - Refers to strange objects or phenomena found in a bamboo garden, suggesting a mysterious or supernatural event.
  • 空同山人 - A reference to a person from the mythical Kunlun Mountains, often used to denote a hermit or sage.
  • 张秋帛烬 - Refers to a specific event or artifact, possibly related to the burning of silk or cloth.
  • 王絙 - A personal name, likely of a historical or legendary figure.
  • 灵芝 - Refers to a mythical fungus believed to grant immortality or longevity.
  • 马鬼 - Refers to a ghost or spirit associated with horses, often appearing in ghost stories or folklore.
  • 三足鳖 - Refers to a mythical turtle with three feet, a symbol of longevity or wisdom.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 圣瑞 - Refers to a divine sign or auspicious event, often associated with the birth of a great leader or ruler.
  • 仁祖先家于泗，后徙濠梁 - Refers to the early life of Zhu Yuanzhang (the founder of the Ming Dynasty), who was born in Suzhou and later moved to Huaibei.
  • 高皇帝 - The posthumous title of Zhu Yuanzhang, the first emperor of the Ming Dynasty.
  • 太学 - Refers to the Imperial Academy, the highest institution of learning in ancient China.
  • 广业堂 - Refers to a specific hall within the Imperial Academy, associated with academic achievement and success.

Proper Nouns

  • 圣瑞 - A divine sign or auspicious event.
  • 仁祖 - Refers to Zhu Yuanzhang, the founder of the Ming Dynasty.
  • 高皇帝 - The posthumous title of Zhu Yuanzhang.
  • 太学 - The Imperial Academy.
  • 广业堂 - A specific hall within the Imperial Academy.

[6]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 坐不法伏诛 - Punished for illegal acts and executed.
  • 阖门受歼于是 - The entire family was killed there.
  • 如万马腾踏者云 - Like the galloping of ten thousand horses, so it is said.
  • 知天命有在 - Knew that fate was in play.
  • 叹息收兵而止 - Sighed, gathered his troops, and stopped.
  • 后兵败被擒 - Later, his army was defeated and he was captured.
  • 见王 - Met the King.
  • 倘相及何如 - What if I had caught up with you?
  • 臣欲生致使长耳 - I wanted to capture you alive to serve as your attendant.
  • 不然，未可量也 - Otherwise, it would be hard to predict.
  • 王劳而赦之 - The King rewarded and pardoned him.
  • 命掌北平都司事 - Ordered him to take charge of the Beiping Commandery.
  • 后以旧人自疑，经死 - Later, due to suspicion of his old connections, he was executed.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 贡院 - The imperial examination hall, a crucial institution for selecting government officials.
  • 纪纲 - A historical figure who was a high-ranking official during the Ming Dynasty.
  • 燕王 - Refers to Zhu Di, who later became the Yongle Emperor.
  • 元氏旧俗 - Refers to the customs of the Yuan Dynasty, which influenced the early Ming Dynasty.
  • 使长 - A term used in the Yuan Dynasty to refer to an attendant or servant.

Proper Nouns

  • 贡院 - The imperial examination hall.
  • 锦衣指挥纪纲 - Jin Yi Commandant Ji Gang.
  • 文皇帝 - The Hongwu Emperor, founder of the Ming Dynasty.
  • 南京 - The capital of the Ming Dynasty.
  • 平保儿 - General Ping An.
  • 燕王 - Zhu Di, who later became the Yongle Emperor.
  • 小河 - A location where a significant battle took place.
  • 北平 - The capital of the Ming Dynasty, now Beijing.

[7]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 袁太常珙相术之妙 - The marvel of Yuan Taichang Góng's physiognomy, referring to his exceptional skill in physiognomy.
  • 胜国时 - In the time of the previous dynasty, referring to the Yuan Dynasty before the Ming.
  • 姚少师广孝 - Yáo Shàoshī Guǎngxiào, a historical figure known as the Grand Tutor of the Yongle Emperor.
  • 缁流 - A monk or a member of the Buddhist clergy, referring to Yáo's status as a monk.
  • 寓嵩山寺 - Residing at Songshan Temple, indicating his place of residence.
  • 匡辅器 - A person capable of assisting and supporting, referring to someone with the potential to be a great helper.
  • 刘秉忠之俦 - Comparable to Liu Bingzhong, referring to a historical figure known for his wisdom and political acumen.
  • 高僧 - A highly respected monk, indicating Yáo's status.
  • 燕邸 - The Prince of Yan's residence, referring to the future Yongle Emperor.
  • 预密谋 - Participated in secret planning, indicating Yáo's involvement in the prince's plans.
  • 托以珙名隶尺籍 - Claimed that Góng was listed in the military register, a pretext to bring Góng to the prince.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 方传唱进士名 - '方传唱' indicates the ongoing action of announcing, and '进士名' is the object being announced.
  • 自谓当在列 - '自谓' indicates the speaker's self-assessment, and '当在列' indicates their expectation of success.

  • 欲取戴之 - '欲取戴之' indicates the speaker's desire to take and wear the object.
  • 旁有人止之曰 - '旁有人' indicates the presence of someone nearby, and '止之曰' indicates that this person stopped the speaker and spoke.

  • 予此来方物色其人 - '予此来' indicates the time frame, and '方物色其人' indicates the speaker's ongoing search for the person.

  • 盖先曾大父讳与宣同音而旁加王也 - Explains the connection between the names, indicating that the great-grandfather's name had the same pronunciation as 'Xuan' but added a 'wang' on the side.

  • 自孕而生一麟 - A literal expression meaning 'conceived itself and gave birth to a qilin.' This is an unusual and mythological expression, suggesting the cow gave birth without a male partner.
  • 云雾滃合 - A poetic or descriptive phrase meaning 'clouds and mist gathered thickly.' '滃' suggests a dense or heavy accumulation.
  • 红光满室 - 'Red light filled the room,' a common expression in classical texts to describe a miraculous or divine event.
  • 其形畧如鹿 - 'Its form was roughly like that of a deer.' '畧' means 'roughly' or 'approximately.'
  • 蹄及尾皆如牛 - 'Its hooves and tail were like those of a cow.' This indicates a hybrid animal with features of both a deer and a cow.
  • 周身有麟 - 'All over its body were scales or patterns resembling a qilin.' '麟' refers to the qilin, a mythical chimeric creature.
  • 额上一骨锐坚 - 'A sharp, firm bone on its forehead.' This likely refers to the qilin's horn.
  • 隐起肉间 - 'Rising slightly from the flesh.' This suggests that the horn is not fully developed or protruding.
  • 盖其角也 - 'This was its horn.' '盖' is a modal particle used to explain or clarify.
  • 初出胎跳跃 - 'When it first emerged from the womb, it leaped.' This describes the animal’s immediate movement upon birth.
  • 人不能制 - 'People could not control it.' This suggests the creature was powerful or magical.

  • 击之，伤右胁而死 - 'They struck it, and it was wounded on the right side and died.' This indicates the people's attempt to kill it.
  • 后有识其为麟者 - 'Later, someone recognized it as a qilin.' '识' means to recognize or identify.

  • 予外祖参议公佐郡日 - 'When my maternal grandfather, the Canshi (参议), was serving as the prefect of the county.' '参议' is a title of an official.
  • 常命取观 - 'He often ordered it to be brought for viewing.' This implies that the dried remains were displayed or examined.
  • 岁久骨肉已化 - 'After many years, the bones and flesh had decomposed.' '岁久' means 'after many years.'
  • 惟存空皮，麟亦间有脱落 - 'Only the empty skin remained, and occasionally the scales (麟) fell off.' '麟' here may refer to the scales or patterns on the qilin's body.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sentence '解库中失金首饰，美人指令于城西黄牛坊钱肆中寻之，盗者以易钱若干去矣。' describes a series of events: the loss of gold jewelry in a pawnshop, the beauty's order to search for it in a specific location, and the thief's action of exchanging it for money and leaving. The sentence is syntactically clear but requires careful translation to maintain the sequence of events.
  • The sentence '诘朝往访焉，物宛然在，径取以归，主人者徒瞪目视而已。' describes the next day's visit, the discovery that the object was still there, and the owner's reaction. The sentence is elliptical but can be expanded for clarity.
  • The sentence '鏊尝与人有争，稍不胜，其人或无故僵仆，或以他事横被折辱' describes Ao's disputes with others and the consequences, which are somewhat ambiguous but can be clarified in translation.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 誠踈於禮 - 'Truly a breach of etiquette.' 踈 here means 'to be lacking' or 'to fall short of.'
  • 已而即席坐 - 'Afterward, he sat at the table.' 已而 means 'after a while' or 'shortly after.'

  • 二仆捧牙盘 - 'Two servants carried a jade tray.' 牙盘 is a type of ornate tray, often made of jade or ivory, used for presenting gifts.
  • 置金银缯锦其中以馈 - 'They placed gold, silver, silk, and brocade within it as a gift.' 金银缯锦 are valuable items used as gifts or bribes.
  • 胥辞谢再三乃受 - 'The official refused many times before accepting.' 胥 refers to a government official or clerk.
  • 遂告归 - 'Then he took his leave.' 遂 means 'then' or 'therefore.'
  • 命二生送胥，更由他门以出 - 'He ordered the two youths to escort the official, and to exit through a different gate.' 更由他门 indicates a different or secret exit.
  • 路经一室，见有绷系树上者 - 'On the way, he passed a room and saw someone bound to a tree.' 绷系 means bound or tied up.
  • 谛视则其亲家也 - 'Upon close inspection, it was his relative (in-law).' 亲家 refers to a relative by marriage, such as a brother-in-law or sister-in-law.
  • 好为缓颊也 - 'I hope you will speak on my behalf.' 缓颊 is a euphemism for interceding or pleading on someone’s behalf.
  • 诘旦往候 - 'The next morning, he went to visit.' 诘旦 is a formal term for the next day or early morning.
  • 方病笃 - 'Was just then in a critical condition.' 病笃 means 'illness is severe.'
  • 见阎君 - 'He saw the Lord of Yama.' 阎君 is the Chinese name for Yama, the god of death in Buddhist cosmology.
  • 垂陷囹圄 - 'Was about to fall into prison.' 圉圄 is a term for prison or jail.
  • 赖公为二子言 - 'Thanks to you, my two sons spoke on my behalf.' 赖 means 'thanks to' or 'because of.'
  • 是后二生讫不复来矣 - 'After that, the two youths never came again.' 讫不复来 means 'never came again.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 老翁方巾皂袍 - This attire is characteristic of a scholar-official or gentleman from the literati class in classical China. The 方巾 (square headpiece) and 皂袍 (black robe) were traditional clothing for scholars and officials.
  • 二生 - Likely refers to two young men, possibly students or attendants. In classical Chinese fiction, such characters often serve as hosts or guides in supernatural or dream sequences.
  • 胥 - A term for a government official or clerk, often in the context of local administration or bureaucracy. In this context, 胥 may be a low-ranking official or a government agent.
  • 亲家 - Refers to a relative by marriage, such as a brother-in-law or sister-in-law, indicating a social relationship rather than a blood relation.
  • 阎君 - 阎君 or Yanwang, the Chinese counterpart of Yama, the god of death in Buddhist cosmology. This is a reference to the underworld or the afterlife.
  • 囹圄 - A term for prison or jail, often used metaphorically in classical Chinese to refer to the afterlife or punishment in the underworld.
  • 冥府 - Mingfu, the Chinese concept of the underworld or the realm of the dead. This is a common setting in Chinese ghost stories and supernatural tales.
  • 二生为阎君之子 - The revelation that the two youths are the sons of Yanwang (the Lord of Death) indicates that the entire banquet and encounter took place in the afterlife or in a supernatural realm.

Proper Nouns

  • 阎君 - Yan Jun or Yanwang, the Chinese god of death. This is a proper name in the context of Chinese mythology.
  • 冥府 - Mingfu, the Chinese underworld. This is a proper noun in the context of Chinese cosmology.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 祝氏牧兒 - Zhu's herdsman (姓氏 + 职业). '牧兒' means herdsman or shepherd.
  • 素善飯 - Good at eating or skilled at eating. Could mean the herdsman was known for his hearty appetite.
  • 故不與以戲之 - Intentionally not giving it to him as a joke. Indicates a deliberate act of teasing.
  • 就午食 - To go for lunch or to go for the midday meal.
  • 庖婦 - Cook or cook in the kitchen.
  • 一道士過 - A Taoist passed by. '道士' refers to a Taoist or Daoist practitioner.
  • 大如龍眼 - As big as a dragon eye. Metaphor for a small, round object.
  • 授之曰 - Gave it to him and said.
  • 勿嚼碎 - Do not chew it. Implies the pill should be swallowed whole.
  • 覺腹中充然 - Felt full in the belly. '充然' means feeling full or satisfied.
  • 道人戒曰 - The Taoist warned him and said.
  • 無語人也 - Do not tell anyone. Warning to keep the matter secret.
  • 遂去 - Then left. '遂' means then or subsequently.
  • 暮歸不飡 - Returned at dusk without eating. '暮' means dusk or evening, and '飡' means to eat.
  • 明日亦不饥 - The next day, he also did not feel hungry. '明日' means the next day, and '饥' means hungry.
  • 绝谷者五六日 - Stopped eating for five or six days. '绝谷' means to stop eating food.
  • 白于主人 - Reported to the master. '白' means to report or inform.
  • 主人召儿将鞭之 - The master summoned the child and was about to whip him. '召' means to summon, and '鞭' means to whip or beat.
  • 吐实 - Told the truth. '吐' means to tell or reveal, and '实' means the truth.
  • 主人异之 - The master found it strange. '异' means to find strange or unusual.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 祝氏 - A surname, indicating the herdsman's family name.
  • 海盐 - A place name, likely referring to Haiyan, a county in Zhejiang Province, China.
  • 龙眼 - Dragon eye, a type of fruit. In this context, it is used metaphorically to describe the size of the black pill.
  • 道士 - Taoist, a practitioner of Taoism, a major Chinese philosophical and religious tradition.
  • 绝谷 - Stopping eating, a practice sometimes associated with fasting or asceticism in Chinese culture.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 素善饭 could be interpreted as 'good at eating' or 'skilled at eating,' but it could also mean 'used to eating well' or 'accustomed to a good diet.'
  • 故不与以戏之 could be interpreted as 'intentionally not giving it to him as a joke' or 'deliberately withholding it to tease him.'
  • 觉腹中充然 could be interpreted as 'felt full in the belly' or 'felt satisfied in the belly.'
  • 绝谷者五六日 could be interpreted as 'stopped eating for five or six days' or 'went without food for five or six days.'

Proper Nouns

  • 祝氏 - A surname.
  • 海盐 - A place name.
  • 龙眼 - A type of fruit, used metaphorically in this context.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 黄鼠豺数十，逐逐成羣 - Dozens of yellow rats and jackals, gathering in packs; describing a large number of animals coming together in groups.
  • 白昼公出 - Come out in public during the day; indicating that the animals are active openly during daylight hours.
  • 搏食鸡畜，囓坏衣案 - Attack and eat chickens and livestock, gnawing and damaging clothes and tables; describing the destructive behavior of the animals.
  • 占卜云 - The divination said; indicating a prophetic or supernatural explanation.
  • 是怪汴将讨宗茂符治之 - This is the spirit of Bian, who will seek out Master Zhang's talismans to deal with it; referring to a supernatural entity named Bian who will try to counter Zhang’s magic.
  • 未暇便说 - Not yet having time to explain; indicating that the divination result was not fully elaborated.
  • 拱立于前者 - Standing in a respectful posture in front of him; indicating a respectful or reverent stance.

  • 拜诉曰 - Bowed and said; indicating a respectful address.
  • 吾辈与陈举人家有夙寃 - We have a long-standing grudge with the family of the scholar-official Chen; indicating a supernatural vendetta.
  • 欲报之 - We wish to avenge it; expressing the creature’s motive.
  • 彼来求师，师无豫吾事 - He has come to seek the master, but the master has no involvement in our matter; indicating that Zhang is not responsible for the creatures’ actions.
  • 叱之，忽不见 - He scolded it, and it suddenly disappeared; describing the magical response.
  • 为咒水祓除，书符镇之 - He performed a ritual with magical water to purify and a talisman to subdue them; referring to Daoist purification and protective practices.
  • 怪遂绝 - The supernatural occurrences ceased; indicating the problem was resolved.
  • 无疾坐逝 - Died without illness, sitting calmly; a phrase used to describe a peaceful and virtuous death, common in Daoist biographies.
  • 为近时羽流称首 - Was the most revered among contemporary Daoist practitioners; indicating his high status in the Daoist community.
  • 姨夫陈崇仁说 - Said by the maternal uncle Chen Chongren; indicating the source of the story is a relative of the author.

  • 黄犬生双角 - A yellow dog was born with two horns; a physical anomaly.
  • 冠尾忽长，遂化为雄 - Its comb and tail suddenly grew long, and it transformed into a male; describing a gender transformation.
  • 能引吭高鸣 - Could raise its voice and sing loudly; indicating the chicken's new ability.
  • 道官薛明净闻其地一巡检说 - The Daoist official Xue Mingjing heard this from a county magistrate in the area; indicating the source of the second story.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 率諸魚一一飛出港而去 - Literally means 'the fish one by one flew out of the harbor and left.' The phrase '一一' emphasizes the individual action of each fish, while '率' suggests that this was a general phenomenon.
  • 若去而復還 - Literally means 'if they go and return,' indicating a cyclical or repeated behavior.
  • 侵晨出樓門 - Literally means 'at dawn, he went out of the Loumen gate.' '侵晨' is a classical way of saying 'at dawn' or 'early morning.'
  • 以身就泥中展轉數四 - Literally means 'it used its body to roll around several times in the mud.' The phrase '展轉' describes the act of turning or twisting.
  • 起集敗荷葉上 - Literally means 'it then gathered on a withered lotus leaf.' '集' implies a movement or gathering.
  • 物類之相感化固然 - Literally means 'the transformation of things by mutual influence is indeed natural.' '固然' emphasizes the naturalness or inevitability of the phenomenon.

  • 有妇容服妖冶 - Means 'a woman with beautiful and alluring attire.'

  • 曰：「儿此间镇上人，将归母家，日暮不能及。如见留，不敢忘报。」 - Means 'she said, 'I am a local from this town, on my way to my mother's house. It's getting dark, and I can't make it. If you could let me stay, I won't forget to thank you.'
  • 兵拒之，妇不肯去 - Means 'the soldier refused, but the woman wouldn't leave.'
  • 天益暝，请益亟，言辞哀婉 - Means 'as night deepened, she pleaded more urgently, her words filled with sorrow.'
  • 兵不觉应曰：「诺。」 - Means 'the soldier unconsciously replied, 'Yes.'
  • 即留之宿兵所，卧处仅与隔一板 - Means 'he let her stay at the camp, her sleeping place was separated by only a board.'
  • 中夜，妇呼腹痛，娇啼宛转 - Means 'in the middle of the night, the woman cried out in pain, her cries were soft and plaintive.'
  • 兵闻之心动，乃自起煎姜汤与饮 - Means 'the soldier, moved by her cries, got up and made ginger soup for her to drink.'
  • 稍逼就之，妇殊不羞拒，兵遂与狎，绸缪倾倒，良以为奇遇也 - Means 'he approached her, and she did not shy away. They became intimate, and the soldier considered it a wonderful encounter.'
  • 五更，天大雪，妇辞归 - Means 'at the fifth watch of the night, it started snowing heavily, and the woman said she would return.'
  • 谓兵曰：「儿家去此不远，君有心者，儿今夜当复来耳。」 - Means 'she said to the soldier, 'My home is not far from here. If you are interested, I will come back tonight.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • The Hongzhi period was a time of relative peace and prosperity in the Ming Dynasty.
  • Changshu County was a significant area in Jiangsu Province, known for its agricultural and cultural contributions.
  • The military garrison in Hangzhou was an important administrative and military center during the Ming Dynasty.
  • Canal ships were crucial for transporting grain and other goods along the Grand Canal, a vital artery for the economy.
  • Gǔtíng Town was a small town in the region, likely known for its local customs and folklore.
  • Foxes are often associated with supernatural beings or spirits in Chinese folklore, particularly in tales of fox spirits (hú xiān).

  • 谷亭镇 - Gǔtíng Town, a small town in the region.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 以绣枕顶一副，并所市猪肝肺遗之，云：「子可持归作■〈羔上火下〉奉母也。」 - This sentence is a compound structure where the main action is giving items, followed by a speech. The speech itself is a command or suggestion.
  • 兵寝，日晏未起 - This sentence describes the soldier's state, where he is sleeping and does not wake up until late.
  • 时舟中诸人皆知之 - This sentence indicates that everyone on the boat knew about the soldier's condition.
  • 或起循其去路，视积雪中乃有兽迹数十 - This sentence describes the actions of the boat's occupants, who followed the soldier's path and found animal tracks in the snow.
  • 共计曰：「彼美而尤，且侵夜来，未明辄去，宁知非妖乎？」 - This sentence is a collective statement, where the group expresses their suspicion that the soldier might be a demon.
  • 呼兵起讯之，初尚抵讳，引登岸，指雪迹示焉，乃大惊吐实 - This sentence describes a series of actions: calling the soldier, questioning him, leading him to the shore, pointing out the snow tracks, and finally, the soldier revealing the truth.
  • 相与到镇上访之，居人或云：「此地有数百年老狐，变幻惑人多矣。君所遭者，将无是乎！」 - This sentence describes the group visiting the town and hearing from the locals about a centuries-old fox that often tricks people.
  • 亟返舟，集众持器械、薪火而行 - This sentence describes the group's immediate return to the boat and their preparation for a hunt.
  • 逐其迹至野外，转入幽邃，迹穷，见大树可数抱，中穿一穴 - This sentence describes the group following the tracks to a remote area, where they find a large tree with a hole in it.
  • 众喜曰：「此必狐窟也。」环而围之，投薪穴中，烧■〈艹执火，上中下〉良久，一狐突烟而出，众格杀之 - This sentence describes the group's discovery of the fox den, their actions to trap and kill the fox, and the fox's emergence.
  • 兵神痴旬日，乃平复 - This sentence describes the soldier's recovery from his daze.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 尋為部民告讦 - Soon after, the local people accused him. '尋' means 'soon after,' and '告讦' means 'to accuse or denounce.'
  • 以為妖术惑众 - Accusing him of using witchcraft to deceive the people. '妖术' refers to 'witchcraft' or 'sorcery,' and '惑众' means 'to deceive the people.'
  • 有诏逮梓人下吏治之 - An imperial decree was issued to arrest the carpenter and bring him to the officials for punishment. '有诏' means 'an imperial decree,' '逮' means 'to arrest,' '下吏' means 'to bring before the officials,' and '治之' means 'to punish.'
  • 数月瘐死狱中 - He died of illness in prison after several months. '瘐死' means 'to die of illness in prison,' and '狱中' means 'in prison.'
  • 弃其尸，家人发丧成服 - His body was discarded, and his family mourned and wore mourning clothes. '弃其尸' means 'to discard his body,' '发丧' means 'to mourn,' and '成服' means 'to wear mourning clothes.'
  • 一夜忽闻扣门声 - One night, they suddenly heard knocking on the door. '忽闻' means 'suddenly heard,' and '扣门声' means 'knocking on the door.'
  • 答曰：「身是梓人也。」 - He replied, 'I am the carpenter.' '身是' means 'I am,' and '梓人' refers to 'the carpenter.'
  • 吾实以间逃去，云死者诈也，勿疑 - I actually escaped in the meantime. They said I was dead, but don't doubt it. '吾实' means 'I actually,' '以间逃去' means 'escaped in the meantime,' '云死者诈也' means 'they said I was dead,' and '勿疑' means 'don't doubt it.'
  • 家人不信，谓鬼衣无缝，验之乃不然 - The family didn't believe him, saying that ghosts' clothes have no seams, but upon checking, it wasn't so. '不信' means 'didn't believe,' '谓鬼衣无缝' means 'saying that ghosts' clothes have no seams,' and '验之乃不然' means 'upon checking, it wasn't so.'
  • 遂内之 - So they let him in. '内之' means 'let him in.'
  • 梓人不自晦匿 - The carpenter did not hide himself. '不自晦匿' means 'did not hide himself.'
  • 日与故旧游宴 - He daily socialized with his old friends. '日与' means 'daily with,' '故旧' means 'old friends,' and '游宴' means 'to socialize.'
  • 或泛舟不用柁楫，逆水而上以为乐 - Sometimes he would sail a boat without a rudder or oars, going upstream against the current for fun. '泛舟' means 'to sail a boat,' '不用柁楫' means 'without a rudder or oars,' '逆水而上' means 'going upstream,' and '以为乐' means 'for fun.'
  • 里人刘氏，其怨家也 - A local man named Liu, who was his enemy. '里人' means 'a local man,' '刘氏' means 'Liu,' and '其怨家也' means 'who was his enemy.'
  • 执而絷之，白知州伍芳，请奏闻 - He seized him and tied him up, then reported to the governor Wu Fang, asking for a report to be made to the emperor. '执而絷之' means 'seized him and tied him up,' '白知州伍芳' means 'reported to the governor Wu Fang,' and '请奏闻' means 'asked for a report to be made to the emperor.'
  • 芳异其事 - Governor Wu Fang found the matter strange. '异其事' means 'found the matter strange.'
  • 刘遂诣阙告之 - Liu then went to the imperial court to report it. '诣阙' means 'went to the imperial court,' and '告之' means 'to report it.'
  • 朝命法官来州推按 - The imperial court sent a judge to the prefecture to investigate. '朝命' means 'the imperial court ordered,' '法官' means 'judge,' '来州' means 'to the prefecture,' and '推按' means 'to investigate.'
  • 一日忽失梓人所在 - One day, the carpenter suddenly disappeared. '忽失' means 'suddenly disappeared,' and '所在' means 'his whereabouts.'
  • 但存铁索而已 - Only a chain remained. '但存' means 'only remained,' and '铁索' means 'a chain.'
  • 刘无以自明 - Liu had no way to prove himself. '无以' means 'had no way,' and '自明' means 'to prove himself.'
  • 竟坐欺罔得重谴 - He was ultimately punished severely for false accusations. '竟坐' means 'ultimately was punished,' '欺罔' means 'false accusations,' and '得重谴' means 'received severe punishment.'
  • 而梓人自是不复见云 - And the carpenter was never seen again. '自是' means 'from then on,' '不复见' means 'was never seen again,' and '云' means 'it is said.'
  • 梓人自号七十一峯道人 - The carpenter called himself the 'Seventy-One Peaks Hermit.' '自号' means 'called himself,' and '七十一峯道人' means 'Seventy-One Peaks Hermit.'
  • 词翰遒逸可观 - His writings were forceful and graceful, worth reading. '词翰' means 'writings,' '遒逸' means 'forceful and graceful,' and '可观' means 'worth reading.'
  • 吴用藏其所制游太山歌一纸 - Wu Yong kept one copy of his poem 'Traveling on Mount Tai.' '藏' means 'kept,' '所制' means 'his creation,' '游太山歌' means 'poem 'Traveling on Mount Tai,' and '一纸' means 'one copy.'
  • 予尝见之 - I have seen it before. '尝见' means 'have seen before.'

  • 梓人 - Refers to a carpenter or artisan, a common profession in ancient China.

  • 妖术 - Refers to witchcraft or sorcery, which was often feared and condemned in ancient Chinese society.
  • 诏 - Refers to an imperial decree, indicating the involvement of the imperial court in the story.
  • 吏 - Refers to officials, indicating the legal and administrative context of the story.
  • 狱 - Refers to prison, indicating the legal punishment of the carpenter.
  • 发丧成服 - Refers to the mourning rituals in ancient Chinese culture, where family members would wear mourning clothes.
  • 鬼 - Refers to ghosts, indicating the supernatural elements in the story.
  • 鬼衣无缝 - A common belief in ancient China that ghosts' clothes have no seams, used here to challenge the carpenter's claim of being a ghost.
  • 故旧 - Refers to old friends or acquaintances, indicating the social network of the carpenter.
  • 泛舟 - Refers to sailing a boat, indicating the carpenter's leisure activities.
  • 柁楫 - Refers to the rudder and oars of a boat, indicating the carpenter's unusual method of sailing.
  • 逆水而上 - Refers to going upstream against the current, indicating the carpenter's adventurous spirit.
  • 知州 - Refers to a governor, indicating the local administrative authority.
  • 伍芳 - Refers to the name of a specific governor, Wu Fang.
  • 朝 - Refers to the imperial court, indicating the central authority in the story.
  • 法官 - Refers to a judge, indicating the legal investigation of the matter.
  • 推按 - Refers to an investigation, indicating the legal process.
  • 欺罔 - Refers to false accusations, indicating the legal consequences for Liu.
  • 七十一峯道人 - Refers to the carpenter's self-given title, indicating his spiritual or artistic identity.
  • 词翰 - Refers to writings or literary works, indicating the carpenter's artistic talents.
  • 游太山歌 - Refers to a specific poem, indicating the carpenter's literary creation.
  • 吴用 - Refers to the name of a person who kept the poem, indicating the transmission of the carpenter's work.
  • 予 - Refers to the narrator or the person telling the story, indicating the source of the account.

  • 杖殺之 - '杖殺' is a method of execution using a bamboo rod or stick, often used in imperial China for lesser crimes. '之' refers to the person being executed.

  • 乃厚劳其人云 - And then greatly rewarded the person; showing the emperor's gratitude and reward.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 湿 - In traditional Chinese medicine, '湿' refers to a condition caused by dampness or humidity, often associated with digestive or joint issues.
  • 仁、宣两朝 - The reigns of Emperor Renzong (1022-1063) and Emperor Xuangzong (1425-1435) of the Song and Ming dynasties, respectively.
  • 御药房 - The imperial pharmacy, a specialized institution for preparing and dispensing medicines to the emperor and other high-ranking officials.
  • 草泽医 - A village or folk doctor, often outside the formal medical establishment.
  • 甘草 - A common herb in traditional Chinese medicine known for its ability to harmonize other herbs and treat various conditions.
  • 晨饔 - Breakfast, a meal taken in the morning.
  • 厚劳其人 - To reward someone generously, often with gifts or promotions.

Proper Nouns

  • 仁、宣两朝 - The reigns of Emperor Renzong and Emperor Xuangzong.
  • 宣宗 - Emperor Xuangzong of the Ming dynasty.
  • 寅 - The person who experienced the illness and was treated.
  • 甘草 - A specific herb in traditional Chinese medicine.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 匠者王某夜歸 - Classical phrase structure meaning 'a craftsman named Wang returned home at night.'
  • 青衣白束腰，如隶卒状 - Describes a person in a blue robe and white sash, resembling a '隶卒' (official clerk or low-ranking official).
  • 君亦思酒食乎？ - Polite question meaning 'Do you also wish for food and drink?' with classical particle '乎' for questioning tone.
  • 君姑留此 - 'Please stay here for a moment' or 'You may stay here for now.' '姑' expresses a temporary or provisional suggestion.
  • 了少公事也 - 'To settle a small official matter.' '了' here means 'to finish' or 'to complete.'
  • 匠即取旋纳诸柴积中 - 'The craftsman immediately took the wine jar and placed it among the firewood pile.' '纳诸柴积中' is a classical construction.
  • 其行如飞 - Metaphor meaning 'his movement was as swift as flight,' indicating supernatural speed.
  • 始悟其为冥卒也 - 'He finally realized that it was a '冥卒' (a soul-guide or official of the underworld).'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 黄村 - A village name, likely located near Wu Mountain. In Classical Chinese texts, specific place names are often used without further elaboration.
  • 隶卒 - A term referring to a low-ranking official or clerk, or in some contexts, a soldier or servant.
  • 旋酒 - A term for a 'small jar of wine,' likely a 'xuan' (旋), a type of vessel used in ancient China for storing or carrying wine.
  • 熟鸡 - A 'roasted chicken' or 'cooked chicken,' likely a meal offering or a common food item.
  • 祭五圣 - 'To sacrifice to the Five Saints' or 'Five Deities.' Refers to a traditional religious or ancestral rite in Chinese culture.
  • 冥卒 - 'Soul-guide' or 'underworld official,' a figure from Chinese Taoist or Buddhist cosmology who assists in the journey of the soul after death.
  • The phrase '负之而去' refers to 'to carry him away,' suggesting the transportation of a soul or corpse by supernatural means, a common theme in Chinese folk narratives.

Proper Nouns

  • 黄村 - Huangcun (a village name), likely a real or fictional place near Wu Mountain.
  • 吴山 - Wu Mountain, a geographical feature, possibly in Zhejiang Province.
  • 王某 - Mr. Wang, the name of the craftsman. A generic name used in Classical Chinese to refer to an unnamed character.
  • 旋酒 - Xuan wine, a term for a small jar of wine. The term '旋' may refer to a specific type of vessel.
  • 五圣 - The Five Saints, a term that could refer to five deities, ancestors, or sages. In this context, it refers to deities or spirits worshipped during a ritual.
  • 冥卒 - Soul-guide or underworld official, a supernatural being in Chinese cosmology.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 犬精 - Refers to a dog spirit or a dog that has been transformed into a human-like being, often associated with supernatural abilities.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 成化中 - Refers to the Chenghua period of the Ming Dynasty (1465–1487), a time of relative stability and cultural flourishing.
  • 马长史 - 'Ma, the Chief Magistrate' (长史 was a title for a high-ranking official, often used for the chief administrator of a county or region).

  • 咒诅于神 - A religious or supernatural act, invoking divine wrath or intervention.

  • 控诉 - To bring a complaint or legal case, here used in a supernatural context.
  • 腰痛 - A physical ailment, often used in Chinese ghost stories as a sign of impending death or supernatural retribution.
  • 里妪 - A village woman or elderly woman from the neighborhood, often used as a witness or narrator in folk tales.
